# Transcrição: 05-Reduce CPM with High Page Quality Scores 

- **Fonte:** 05-Reduce CPM with High Page Quality Scores 
- **Duração:** 10:16
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Now your monthly page satisfaction score is such an important metric because it has an impact on your CPMs, so what you're paying in the auction, how you're presented in the auction, so whether you're getting penalized or not. And if your score drops low enough, you'll lose the ability to advertise because you lose your page. So it's so, so important that you are keeping a close eye on your satisfaction scores in the auction and you're doing something about trying to keep the score high or improving it. So, you know, what I'm going to go through in this section is just give you an idea of the things to look out for and what you can do about it as well. So this page example is an example of a page that is just consistently performing high. So the score is out of five, so 4.9 is pretty much as best as you can get. I mean, some pages do get five and it's really, really hard to keep that, but this is a 4.9 and it's really, really positive to see that on a consistent basis because you know that with a 4.9 score or anything literally above the four mark, you're getting the best auctions, the best CPM, etc. and you're able to grow in a more positive way in your ad account as well. And one thing I would say is keep an eye on this on kind of twice a month basis. Now the scores are generally updated once a month, but you can just keep, you won't know whether it's at the start or the end of the month or mid month. So just check in on this at least twice a month to check on your score, make it part of your process, part of your routine to keep an eye on. So here, for example, we've got a 4.9, which is great. We can go into different parts of this and look at where people are grading us in particular. So product quality is the biggest factor that came back. So when Facebook see the sale come in and they know that you're an e-com store, they will send a survey out to that user in their newsfeed asking them to provide feedback about your brand or service. And within that, they'll get you to grade things as well. So for example, product quality, customer service are the things that they're asking to grade on. And for example, on this particular page, product quality is the thing that people most gave the positive feedback for. So then, you know, that's all great. There's always the positive and the negative that you have to keep an eye on as well. So here, for example, there was some negative feedback. Well, Facebook won't tell you is how many, but at least that tells me that even though my score is super high, the reason it's not a five is that there are at least one person that did not receive their item. Now, you might look at this and say, you know, that's quite harsh or you might just say that that's fine, that's fair enough. Now if someone didn't receive the item, what can you do about that?

## [03:01 - 06:04]

Now obviously, you want to improve your shipping and things like that. But what I mean is if they didn't receive their item by the time Facebook sent them the survey, but they did receive it after what you do, why would suggest and why recommend is don't wait for Facebook to survey your customer. You should be surveying your customer yourself. I use a tool, I use a process called the net promoter score. And what the NPS, the net promoter score allows me to do is survey my customers after they've received the products or service to get feedback from them on how satisfied they are. That's one way of doing it. You could automate it in another way or sending an email a few days after they've received the products or maybe even after they've made the purchase to make sure that they're happy. Because what you don't want is, you know, if you say shipping takes three to five days and they'll some delay and Facebook send the survey after seven days and the person says, well, I haven't received my product yet. It might be a genuine problem that you've already handled, but the user is actually answering it factually. So yes, they haven't actually received their products and that counts against you in your page scores. But what you can do is when you're when you're surveying your customers and you say, we've got this handled, it will be arriving, you know, within one or two days, whatever it is, at least if you're having that conversation ahead of Facebook pushing that survey, you have a higher chance of that person being happier. So if, for example, they've just purchased the item hasn't arrived in five days, like you told them to, they get the survey a few days later, so seven days after the purchase and they haven't received any comms from you and they haven't received the service, they're going to downgrade you based on that. So really be careful about relying on Facebook telling you your score. You should be doing this yourself. This is literally something which a serious business should be doing anyway is to make sure that your customers are happy. So, you know, measuring return on ad spend, click through rate, all your quality metrics in your account is all great. But that doesn't tell you whether the post purchase, the post purchase experience was good. You need to be asking your customers. So don't wait for Facebook to send the survey out. Send something before Facebook do. And if there's a problem, just solve it. Just solve it at any cost. Even if it's a case of just refunding because it was, you know, the customers really, really annoyed and they expected after five days and after seven days, it's not there. Resolve the problem because losing your page or having a low page feedback score is going to literally could literally stop you from advertising. So if running, if running Facebook ads is important to you, if getting low costs and high profitability is important to you, consistency, all the things that we've been talking through in the BPM method, you need to take your page scores seriously.

## [06:04 - 09:06]

So this is an example of a high performing store. Then I'll give you an example of an average store. So this store is at 3.2 right now. This isn't as good as I'd want it to be. I want to get it up to the four threshold. So then I can kind of look down and say, right, you know, what are people commenting the product quality, etc. But although those that were happy talked about the product quality, those that were not happy actually mentioned the product quality and the delivery speed equally. So you know, there's a few things that we can do here. One is go back through our Q&A with the provider, go and look at exactly what the product's quality feedback is. You can have a look at the comments. So Facebook often collect the comments if there are any, or you can go and create a survey asking specifically about this. So what I would do in this case is create a survey for anyone that's purchased, you know, maybe a week or two after I expect delivery to have been made and ask them straight up, what do you think about the product quality? If you didn't like it, you know, tell us the feedback about it. But you need to fix the problem because the sooner you fix it, the quicker you can recover your scores and bring it back as well. And delivery speed is another one as well. So you know, for example, I know through this period right now, we've just had a backlog of orders that we couldn't clear, and it's taken longer for some of the deliveries to get pushed out. So on the one hand, part of it could be out of your control if it is then get it solved. But on the other hand, what you can actually do is to set your delivery speed as well. So for example, you know, by default, Facebook will try and contact people after two weeks of their purchase. But you can actually change this to better customize what you're offering customers. Now, this isn't a hack that, you know, you should just send this, set this as long as you can. It's going to be great because Facebook will also ask, did it arrive when you expected it to as well. So if it is going to take a long time, and that's fine if your delivery time is four weeks, it is what it is. But communicate that in your ad and communicate that in your landing page or wherever it is relevant for people to understand that it's going to take some time to get delivery. Because that again, you know, something as simple as getting the delivery times right and communicating that effectively can impact your page scores and bring it down as well. So a 3.2 level, it's okay, it won't necessarily punish the CPMs, but I may not be winning as many auctions as I should, as if it was above four. So that's that. And you can see the penalty threshold right now is at two. So if it's below two, I'll be penalized.

## [09:06 - 10:16]

And if it drops below one, then this is kind of what happens. So this is an example of a page that's actually been disabled. The page score was at the penalty penalty kind of threshold level of kind of dropping CPMs. And then it plummeted in December, Jan. And then it hasn't recovered since that was specifically around this particular client, I believe, scowled too aggressively in December, and they lost their page because they could not deliver. They, the logistics broke, they were not able to deliver. They had lots of negative feedback from the survey that Facebook sent out, and they lost their page. So, you know, that's, you know, they've come to us asking for help to try and recover it. All you can do is try and request a review. And even if you get the page back, you need to make sure you do not lose it again. Otherwise, you'll end up losing it permanently as well. So this is just something which if you're in the e-com space, do take it seriously because this has a massive, massive knock on impact on your ability, not just to gain profitability and stability, but just enable, in terms of enabling you to run your ads as well.
